perturbation.original_text: Unless the parties agree otherwise, this obligation of confidentiality will expire two (2) years from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless the parties agree otherwise, this obligation of confidentiality will expire one (1) year from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, especially given the rapid pace of technological development.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality period may not be sufficient to prevent misappropriation or reverse engineering, thus undermining the protection afforded by CUTSA, potentially impacting the ability to pursue legal recourse for trade secret violations.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from two years to one year. The Law Explanation states that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. It also says a one-year confidentiality period may be insufficient to prevent misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that the Civil Code (CIV) includes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act under Title 5, Section 3426, meaning it is legally recognized. Because the reduced period may not be sufficient to protect trade secrets under California law, as described in the Perturbation Explanation, the changed text creates an inconsistency violating CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent by a) registered mail; b) tracked overnight carrier or c) email transmission where the recipient specifically replies to acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent exclusively by standard postal mail without tracking or confirmation.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notices to standard postal mail without tracking contradicts modern legal standards for providing adequate notice, especially in California, where electronic communication is widely accepted and often required.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 outlines procedures for service by mail, and modern interpretations favor methods that provide proof of delivery, such as registered or certified mail. Disallowing electronic communication and requiring standard postal mail makes it difficult to prove notice was received, potentially undermining the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by restricting notices to standard postal mail without tracking, directly contradicts the spirit of ensuring adequate notice, especially given the availability of methods providing proof of delivery. The original text allowed for methods like registered mail and email with acknowledgement, providing a record of receipt. The changed text undermines the enforceability of the agreement by making it difficult to prove notice was received. California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, acknowledges service by mail as valid when deposited in a USPS facility. However, it does not preclude or discourage the use of methods that provide proof of delivery, which are favored in modern legal interpretations to ensure enforceability. By exclusively mandating standard mail, the change contradicts the trend towards reliable forms of communication that confirm receipt, thereby increasing the risk of disputes over whether proper notice was given.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its choice of laws rules.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, excluding its choice of laws rules, regardless of the location of either party or the location where the confidential information is used or disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: Choosing Delaware law when the agreement involves a California corporation and the relevant activities occur in California can be problematic if it undermines California's strong public policy regarding trade secrets or other relevant issues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Choice of Law Principles (Contract Law)
perturbation.law_citation: California Choice of Law Principles (Contract Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: California courts will generally enforce choice-of-law provisions unless the chosen law is contrary to a fundamental policy of California. If the application of Delaware law would substantially diminish protections afforded by California law (e.g., in trade secret protection or employee rights), a California court may refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision, especially if California has a materially greater interest in the litigation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CACI No. 303. Breach of Contract - Essential Factual Elements :: California Civil Jury Instructions (CACI) (2025) :: Justia CACI No. 303. Breach of Contract - Essential Factual Elements Judicial Council of California Civil Jury Instructions (2025 edition) Download PDF 303 . Breach of Contract - Essential Factual Elements T o recover damages from [ name of defendant ] for br each of contract, [ name of plaintiff ] must prove all of the following: 1. That [ name of plaintiff ] and [ name of defendant ] entered into a contract; [2. That [ name of plaintiff ] did all, or substantially all, of the significant things that the contract required [him/her/ nonbinary pronoun /it] to do;] [2. [or] [2. That [ name of plaintiff ] was excused from having to [ specify things that plaintiff did not do, e.g., obtain a guarantor on the contract ];] [3. That [ specify occurrence of all conditions required by the contract for [name of defendant]’s performance, e.g., the property was rezoned for residential use ];] [3. [or] [3. That [ specify condition(s) that did not occur ] [was/were] [waived/ excused];] [4. That [ name of defendant ] failed to do something that the contract required [him/her/ nonbinary pronoun /it] to do;] [4. [ or ] [4. That [ name of defendant ] did something that the contract prohibited [him/her/ nonbinary pronoun /it] from doing;] 5. That [ name of plaintiff ] was harmed; and 6. That [ name of defendant ]’s breach of contract was a substantial factor in causing [ name of plaintiff ]’s harm. New September 2003; Revised April 2004, June 2006, December 2010, June 201 1, June 2013, June 2015, December 2016, May 2020 Directions for Use Read this instruction in conjunction with CACI No. 300, Br each of Contract - Intr oduction . Optional elements 2 and 3 both involve conditions precedent. A “condition precedent” is either an act of a party that must be performed or an uncertain event that must happen before the contractual right accrues or the contractual duty arises. ( Stephens & Stephens XII, LLC v . Fireman’ s Fund Ins. Co. (2014) 231 Cal.App.4th 91 1 131, 1 147 [180 Cal.Rptr .3d 683].) Element 2 involves the first kind of condition precedent; an act that must be performed by one party before the other is required to perform. Include the second option if the plaintif f alleges that the plaintif f was excused from having to perform some or all of the contractual conditions. Not every breach of contract by the plaintif f will relieve the defendant of the obligation to perform. The breach must be material; element 2 captures materiality by requiring that the plaintif f have done the significant things that the contract required. Also, the two obligations must be dependent , meaning that the parties specifically bargained that the failure to perform the one relieves the obligation to perform the other . While materiality is generally a question of fact, whether covenants are dependent or independent is a matter of construing the agreement. ( Br own v . Grimes (201 1) 192 Cal.App.4th 265, 277-279 [120 Cal.Rptr .3d 893].) If there is no extrinsic evidence in aid of construction, the question is one of law for the court. ( V er dier v . V erdier (1955) 133 Cal.App.2d 325, 333 [284 P .2d 94].) Therefore, element 2 should not be given unless the court has determined that dependent obligations are involved. If parol evidence is required and a dispute of facts is presented, additional instructions on the disputed facts will be necessary . (See City of Hope National Medical Center v . Genentech, Inc. (2008) 43 Cal.4th 375, 395 [75 Cal.Rptr .3d 333, 181 P .3d 142].) Element 3 involves the second kind of condition precedent; an uncertain event that must happen before contractual duties are triggered. Include the second option if the plaintif f alleges that the defendant agreed to perform even though a condition did not occur . For reasons that the occurrence of a condition may have been excused, see the Restatement Second of Contracts, sect
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Delaware. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate that this change could contradict California law if it undermines California's public policy, such as trade secret protection or employee rights. This suggests that the application of Delaware law may weaken protections afforded by California law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, specifically referencing CACI No. 303 (Breach of Contract - Essential Factual Elements), highlights the importance of adhering to California law in contract disputes within California. Choosing Delaware law could lead to a contradiction if it affects the interpretation and enforcement of contract elements as defined under California law, especially if California has a materially greater interest in the litigation.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly after such termination, all obligations of Participant and Khronos under this Agreement will terminate, and both parties will return or destroy all materials provided by the other party pursuant to th is Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the rights and obligations set forth in Sections 4 (Confidential Information), 5.7 (No Warranty) and 5.8 (Limitation of Liability) will survive termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this agreement, all obligations of Participant and Khronos, including those related to Confidential Information, warranties, and liability, will immediately cease.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating the survival clause for confidentiality obligations contradicts typical trade secret protection principles, which require ongoing confidentiality even after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civ. Code § 3426)
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret. By eliminating the survival clause for confidentiality, the agreement allows for the potential disclosure of trade secrets immediately upon termination, directly contradicting the intent and protections afforded by CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text eliminates the survival clause for confidentiality, warranties, and liability, which directly contradicts the principles of trade secret protection. According to the Law Explanation, California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret. By removing the survival clause, the modified agreement permits potential disclosure upon termination, conflicting with the protections afforded by CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Code, CIV 3426, which introduces the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, thus validating the Law Explanation's reference to CUTSA. Therefore, the altered text creates a legal contradiction because it undermines established trade secret protections.

perturbation.original_text: ALL PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE THAT ALL INFORM ATION PROVIDED UNDE R THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY SPECIFICATIONS AND CONTRIBU TIONS, ARE PROVIDED “AS IS” WITH NO WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR OTHERWISE, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSL Y DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OF MERCHANTABILITY, SECURITY, SATIS FACTORY QUALITY, NONINFRINGEMENT INCLUDING NON-INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, FITNESS FOR ANY PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ERROR-FREE OPERATION, OR ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OTHERWISE ARISING OUT OF ANY PROPOSAL, CONTRIBUTION, SPECIFICATION, OR SAMPLE.
perturbation.changed_text: ALL PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE THAT ALL INFORM ATION PROVIDED UNDE R THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY SPECIFICATIONS AND CONTRIBU TIONS, ARE PROVIDED “AS IS” WITH NO WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR OTHERWISE. ALL WARRANTIES ARE DISCLAIMED TO THE FULLEST EXTENT PERMITTED BY LAW, EXCEPT FOR FRAUDULENT MISREPRESENTATION.
perturbation.explanation: While California law generally allows disclaimers of warranties, a blanket disclaimer that attempts to shield a party from liability for its own intentional misrepresentations (fraud) is against public policy and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1668 states that contracts that directly or indirectly exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud or willful injury to the person or property of another are against public policy. Even with the exception for "fraudulent misrepresentation," the original disclaimer disclaims liability to the maximum extent, which could be interpreted as an attempt to shield intentional misconduct and would likely be deemed unenforceable in California courts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the original disclaimer of all warranties by adding an exception for fraudulent misrepresentation. However, it also states that "all warranties are disclaimed to the fullest extent permitted by law." According to California Civil Code § 1668, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, contracts that exempt someone from responsibility for their own fraud are against public policy. The modified clause, while including an exception, still disclaims warranties to the fullest extent, which could be interpreted as an attempt to shield intentional misconduct, thereby contradicting the law as the initial broad disclaimer could be interpreted to include fraud despite the exception, thus creating a contradiction.
